title: Service-Induced Discontinuities
narrationScript: A part that has been in service writes its history on its own surfaces, and a Level Two inspector's job is to read that handwriting. Manufacturing discontinuities are frozen in place the day a part ships. Service-induced discontinuities grow. They respond to load, temperature, chemistry, and flow, and they show up exactly where those drivers concentrate. So before you inspect in-service hardware, ask three questions. What are the loads. What is the temperature history. And what touches the surface. The answers tell you what to expect and where to find it. Start with mechanical loads and the king of service failures, fatigue. Fatigue is cracking from repeated stress cycles below the yield strength. A crack initiates at a stress riser, a keyway, a fillet, a thread root, a weld toe, even a corrosion pit, and then advances a small step with every cycle. On the surface, a fatigue crack is a tight hairline with no deformation around it. It often announces itself indirectly, with a rust streak that keeps coming back, or a dark line that cuts across machining marks instead of following them. Remember this. Scratches follow the tool path. Cracks follow the stress. If the part has already broken, the fracture face is a logbook. The origin is where it all started. Ratchet marks, which are small radial steps near the origin, tell you several cracks started side by side under a strong stress concentration. Beach marks are crack arrest lines that ripple outward and curve around the origin, so you can follow their curvature backward, straight home to the starting point. The final fracture zone is the rough, crystalline patch where the last ligament of metal tore in one overload event. Read its size like a gauge. A small final zone means the running stress was low and the crack had a long life. A large one means the part was working hard when it let go. Thermal loads come in three speeds. Thermal fatigue is repeated heating and cooling, and it produces a network of fine surface cracks that looks like a dried mud flat. You will hear it called craze cracking or checking, and it lives on surfaces that cycle through big temperature swings, die faces, exhaust components, boiler parts. Thermal shock is one violent temperature change, cold water hitting hot metal, and it produces one or a few brittle looking cracks, often starting at a thick to thin transition. Creep is the slow one, long times under stress at high temperature. Most creep damage stays internal, so look for the indirect signs, bulging, sagging, distortion, and heavy oxide scale, and compare dimensions against the originals. Color is evidence too. Temper colors and scale record where the temperature actually went. Corrosion has a face for every form. Uniform corrosion thins everything evenly and looks deceptively harmless, so judge it by thickness measurement, not by appearance. Pitting hides a deep cavity under a small mouth. Measure pits with a pit depth gauge, because depth, not diameter, is what threatens a pressure boundary. Crevice corrosion attacks where oxygen is starved, under gaskets, washers, lap joints, and deposits. Galvanic corrosion concentrates on the less noble metal at a dissimilar metal joint. Intergranular attack follows grain boundaries and leaves a dull, sugary surface, classically in sensitized stainless heat affected zones. And stress corrosion cracking produces fine, branched cracks like lightning, but only when three things line up at once, a susceptible material, sustained tensile stress, and the specific environment, such as chlorides against austenitic stainless steel. Watch for secondary evidence everywhere, rust streaks weeping from a tight joint, blistered paint, crusty deposits at fasteners. Finally, wear and flow. Abrasive wear and galling score sliding surfaces. Fretting grinds fine reddish brown debris out of clamped joints that move a few microns, and those innocent looking shallow pits are favorite starting points for fatigue cracks. Erosion polishes metal away where flow turns or accelerates, leaving smooth, scalloped thinning on elbow outer radii and just downstream of orifices and control valves. Cavitation hammers spongy honeycomb pits into pump impellers and valve trim where vapor bubbles collapse. Here is the discipline to carry out of this lesson. Map the loads, the temperatures, the chemistry, and the flow path before you pick up the flashlight. Go to the stress risers first. Read shape and location together. And when you find a tight dark line, slow down, clean it, magnify it, and treat it as a crack until interpretation proves otherwise.

sections.heading: The Part Tells You Its History
sections.body:
Manufacturing discontinuities are frozen in place the day the part ships. Service-induced discontinuities are different: they grow, they respond to load and environment, and they concentrate exactly where engineering theory says stress and chemistry concentrate. That makes them predictable — if you know the service.

Before inspecting in-service hardware, get three answers. What are the loads — steady, cyclic, vibration, pressure swings? What is the temperature history — steady heat, thermal cycling, quench events? What touches the surface — product chemistry, seawater, steam, abrasive slurry, a mating part? Each answer maps to a damage family: mechanical loads breed fatigue; thermal loads breed thermal fatigue and thermal shock; chemistry breeds corrosion; flow and contact breed erosion and wear.

Then go straight to the **stress risers**: keyways, fillets, threads, weld toes, snap-ring grooves, corrosion pits, old weld repairs, and mechanical damage like gouges and arc strikes. Cracks initiate where stress concentrates, which means your first ten minutes belong to the geometry, not to the broad open surfaces.

sections.heading: Mechanical Loads: Fatigue
sections.body:
Fatigue is failure by repeated stress cycles at levels below the material's yield strength. A microscopic crack initiates at a stress riser, then advances a tiny step with every cycle until the remaining section can no longer carry the load and tears in one final overload event.

On an in-service surface, a fatigue crack is a **tight, hairline, linear indication** with no deformation around it. It often shows itself indirectly: a rust streak that keeps coming back as the crack weeps, a line that interrupts and crosses machining marks, or a hairline that stays dark after the surrounding surface is cleaned. Scratches follow tool or handling paths; fatigue cracks follow the stress field, usually perpendicular to the principal stress.

If a fractured part comes to you for examination, the face is a logbook. The **origin** sits at the initiation point. **Ratchet marks** — small radial steps near the origin — mean several crack fronts started side by side and merged, a sign of high stress concentration. **Beach marks** are crack-arrest lines that curve around the origin like ripples around a stone; follow their curvature backward and they point home. The **final fracture zone** is rough and crystalline where the last ligament tore. Its size is a load gauge: a small final zone means low running stress and a long propagation life; a large one means the part was heavily loaded when it failed.

sections.caption: Fracture face of a failed shaft: origin at the keyway corner, ratchet marks from multiple initiation sites, beach marks curving around the origin, and the rough final fracture zone opposite.

sections.title: Crack or Scratch?
sections.body: A scratch has displaced metal — burred, raised edges you can feel and see under low-angle light — and it follows a tool or handling path. A crack displaces nothing, may jog or branch as it follows microstructure and stress, and often keeps a dark line after cleaning because it holds oxide or fluid. Verify with a 10x magnifier and grazing light before you write either word. When in doubt, treat it as a crack until interpretation proves otherwise.

sections.heading: Thermal Loads
sections.body:
Thermal mechanisms come in three speeds:

- **Thermal fatigue** — repeated heating and cooling cycles strain the surface until a network of shallow cracks forms. The pattern looks like a dried mud flat — fine cracks intersecting at rough right angles, often called craze cracking or checking. Look on surfaces that cycle through the largest temperature swings: die faces, exhaust components, boiler tubes near soot blowers, ladle linings.
- **Thermal shock** — one rapid, severe temperature change, like cold water hitting hot metal. The result is one or a few brittle-looking cracks, frequently starting at a thick-to-thin transition or an existing notch, with none of the mud-flat networking.
- **Creep** — long times under stress at high temperature. Creep damage stays internal for most of its life, so VT clues are indirect: bulging or swelling, sagging, increased oxide scale, and distortion of once-straight edges. Diameter and sag measurements against original dimensions often tell you more than crack hunting.

Color is evidence too. Temper colors and heavy scale record overheating; map any discoloration and report its boundaries, because it tells the engineer where the temperature actually went.

sections.heading: Corrosion
sections.body:
Corrosion is electrochemical metal loss, and each form has a recognizable face:

- **Uniform corrosion** — general thinning with a rough or scaled surface. It looks harmless precisely because it is even; judge it by thickness measurement, not by appearance.
- **Pitting** — small surface mouths hiding deep cavities. Mouth diameter means little; **depth** is what threatens the pressure boundary, so measure pits with a pit gauge, not your eye. Expect pitting on stainless steels in chloride service and under deposits.
- **Crevice corrosion** — attack where oxygen is starved: under gaskets, washers, bolt heads, lap joints, and dried deposits.
- **Galvanic corrosion** — accelerated attack on the less noble metal at a dissimilar-metal joint; check fastener-to-plate and fitting-to-pipe couples.
- **Intergranular attack** — grain-boundary corrosion, classically in sensitized austenitic stainless heat-affected zones; the surface develops a dull, sugary texture.
- **Stress-corrosion cracking (SCC)** — fine, branched, lightning-pattern cracks that need three ingredients at once: a susceptible material, sustained tensile stress, and the specific environment — chlorides on austenitic stainless, ammonia on brass. SCC is tight; cleaning and magnification are mandatory.

Secondary evidence finds corrosion before you see metal loss: rust staining streaking from a joint, blistered paint, and white or green product crusts at fasteners.

sections.caption: Six service-damage morphologies in cross-section: uniform thinning, pitting, crevice attack, stress-corrosion cracking, erosion at an elbow, and cavitation pitting.

sections.heading: Abrasive Wear, Erosion, and Cavitation
sections.body:
Wear mechanisms remove metal by contact or flow, and location is everything:

- **Abrasive wear and galling** — scoring, smearing, and metal transfer on sliding surfaces; check the load-bearing arc of bushings, ways, and pins.
- **Fretting** — clamped joints with microscopic relative motion grind out fine oxide debris, reddish brown on steel. The pits look shallow and harmless; they are not, because fretting damage is a classic fatigue initiation site. Find it at press fits, bolted flanges, and wire-rope contact points.
- **Erosion** — flowing fluid or entrained particles polish metal away, leaving smooth, scalloped, often shiny thinning. It concentrates where flow changes direction or speeds up: elbow outer radii, tees, downstream of orifices and control valves, pump cutwaters.
- **Cavitation** — vapor bubbles collapse against the surface and hammer out clusters of jagged, spongy pits, like a honeycomb. Classic on pump impeller low-pressure faces, valve trim, and diesel-liner water jackets.

Map the flow path and the contact points before you inspect, and you will walk directly to the damage instead of finding it by accident.

sections.caption: Service-induced damage quick reference.
rows: Tight hairline crack; may weep rust; crosses machining marks
Keyways, fillets, threads, weld toes, pits
Die faces, exhaust parts, cyclic hot surfaces
Single brittle crack at a transition
Stainless near chlorides; brass near ammonia
Elbow outer radii; downstream of orifices and valves
Impellers, valve trim, liner water side
Press fits, bolted joints, rope contacts

figures.title: Fatigue Fracture Face of a Failed Pump Shaft

interactive.title: Find the Fatigue Origin
interactive.intro: A pump shaft fractured in service and the fracture face is on your bench. Reading the face backward tells you where the crack started — and the origin is where the root cause lives. Click the region that marks the crack origin.

regions.label: Keyway corner with ratchet marks
regions.feedback: Correct. The keyway corner is the stress riser, and the ratchet marks converging here mean several crack fronts initiated side by side and merged. This is the origin — the place to investigate for root cause: sharp corner radius, fretting, or a machining defect.

regions.label: Beach-marked propagation zone
regions.feedback: Not the origin — but it points to it. Beach marks are crack-arrest lines that curve around the initiation point like ripples around a stone. Follow their centers of curvature back up toward the keyway.

regions.label: Final fracture zone
regions.feedback: This rough, crystalline patch is where the last ligament of metal tore in overload — the END of the story, farthest from the origin. Its relatively small size says the running stress was moderate and the crack grew for a long time.

regions.label: Machined outside surface
regions.feedback: The OD here is smooth with no secondary cracking. Fatigue origins live at geometric stress risers — and on this shaft the dominant riser is the keyway, not the plain machined surface.

checkQuestions.question: On an opened fatigue fracture face, beach marks are best described as:
options: Lines where the crack front paused or changed, curving around and pointing back to the origin
The roughest region of the face, marking the final overload
Corrosion deposits unrelated to crack growth
Machining marks left over from manufacture
checkQuestions.explanation: Beach marks are crack-arrest lines formed as the fatigue crack advanced and paused. They curve around the origin, so tracing them backward locates the initiation site.

checkQuestions.question: Which measurement matters most when evaluating a corrosion pit on a pressure boundary?
options: Mouth diameter
Color of the corrosion product
Pit depth compared with the remaining wall thickness
Number of pits per square foot, regardless of size
checkQuestions.explanation: A pit with a small mouth can hide a deep cavity, and the remaining ligament of sound metal is what holds the pressure. Measure depth with a pit gauge rather than judging by the opening.

checkQuestions.question: During an internal inspection of piping, where is erosion damage most likely to concentrate?
options: Long straight runs at mid-span
The pipe exterior under insulation
Outer radius of elbows and just downstream of orifices and control valves
Uniformly around the full circumference everywhere
checkQuestions.explanation: Erosion concentrates where flow changes direction or accelerates, which is why elbow outer radii and the zones immediately downstream of flow restrictions thin first.

checkQuestions.question: Stress-corrosion cracking requires which combination?
options: Cyclic loading, low temperature, and coating damage
A susceptible material, sustained tensile stress, and a specific corrosive environment — all at the same time
High-velocity flow and entrained abrasive particles
Dissimilar metals in electrical contact
checkQuestions.explanation: SCC is a three-ingredient mechanism: remove the susceptible alloy, the tensile stress, or the specific environment and the branched cracking stops.

checkQuestions.question: You find shallow pits and fine reddish-brown debris at a bolted joint. Why does this finding deserve more than a cosmetic note?
options: It means the joint was recently disassembled
It indicates the bolts were overtorqued at assembly
It only matters on aluminum parts
Fretting damage creates stress risers that commonly initiate fatigue cracks
checkQuestions.explanation: Red-brown oxide debris at a clamped joint is the signature of fretting, and fretting pits are classic fatigue initiation sites — report them and flag the joint for engineering review.

references: CP-105-2024 VT Level II Topical Outline §7.3 (7.3.1–7.3.4)
ASTM E1316, Standard Terminology for Nondestructive Examinations (service-damage terms)
In-service inspection practice in codes such as API 510/570 (generic context for damage location)
